text: The surgeon lifts a large fibroid, approximately eight to nine centimeters, located behind the uterus. A chemical is injected at the junction of the fibroid and the uterus, causing the uterus to appear white, indicating reduced blood supply to enhance surgical safety.

The surgeon cuts the outer capsule of the fibroid, revealing the fibroid beneath. A myoma screw is inserted to apply traction, allowing the surgeon to peel off the capsule, progressively exposing more of the fibroid.

As I said, it looks very simple but takes years and years of our training to actually get there. It's almost like watching someone play a video game and feel that it's very simple to play that game until you play it yourself. And of course it's not just about doing the surgery, it's also about making sure that there are no risks, there are no complications and making it very safe for the patient.

The surgeon uses an electric current to cut the remaining fibers of a large fibroid from the uterus on the right side. The fibroid is successfully detached and removed. Another smaller fibroid located behind the uterus is also excised and removed.

The surgeon observes the large posterior flap of the fibroid capsule, noting the significant gap that needs closure. The posterior flap is trimmed to ensure it fits well with the anterior flap, eliminating excess tissue.
The surgeon stitches the outer layer of the uterine capsule, having already completed an inner layer to eliminate dead spaces and prevent blood collection between the capsule and the uterus.

The surgeon cleans the surgical field and identifies a small fibroid, approximately two to three centimeters, located on top of the uterus. Using the same technique, the outer capsule is incised, and the fibroid is pulled and peeled away. The fibroid bed is then sutured to ensure the margins are opposed, leaving no dead spaces, promoting proper healing.

The surgeon uses a morcellator to shred a large fibroid inside the abdominal cavity into small pieces. The morcellator's blade rotates, cutting long threads of the fibroid capsule, which are then extracted through a small incision measuring 1 to 1.3 centimeters. The procedure is performed with precision to ensure the sharp blade does not contact any surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses bulky instruments to cut out chunks of the fibroid, progressively reducing its size. The fibroid is visibly smaller, and the uterus returns to its normal shape after the removal of the majority of the large fibroids.

Again, as I've said in all my other videos, we have to clean up the uterus, ensure that the peritoneal cavity, the uterus, the ovaries and fallopian tubes and the tummy is absolutely cleaned of all the blood, all the debris, all the small little tissues. So we leave a very, very clean pelvis behind at the end of surgery. So it's washed repeatedly with normal saline. We wait for three months, perform a tubal test on this lady, do an ultrasound just to see how the uterus is looking and quite likely after three months when she tries to conceive naturally, she'll be quite successful in doing so. Of course, subject to the condition that she has or her husband has no other major problems.
